---
title: "Multimodal NGS analysis of gene expression and chromatin state in olfactory
  epithelium"
subtitle: "Day 2 — Running the 3t-seq pipeline for RNA-seq data processing"
author: "Lisa Korneeva"
date: today
format:
  html:
    grid:
      body-width: 1000px
    self-contained: true
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    toc-depth: 3
    theme:
      - flatly
      - custom.scss
    highlight-style: github
    code-fold: show
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, eval = FALSE)
```

---

# Overview

Welcome to Day 2! 

Yesterday we:

- Explored the published dataset and found our samples on **NCBI GEO**
- Set up the computational environment (conda, Nextflow)
- Downloaded the raw **RNA-seq** and **ChIP-seq** data using `nf-core/fetchngs`
- Renamed the downloaded files to meaningful sample names

Today we will:

- Clone, configure and launch the **3t-seq pipeline** to process the RNA-seq data (`fastq` files)

---

# 1. Introduction to 3t-seq

## 1.1 What is 3t-seq?

The **3t-seq pipeline** is a Snakemake-based RNA-seq analysis workflow. It was designed for the analysis of **transposable element (TE), tRNA and transcript** expression analysis. It performs all the standard steps
of a typical RNA-seq analysis pipeline — from raw read processing to differential
expression analysis.

For this course, we will use it for standard RNA-seq processing of our olfactory
epithelium dataset. The TE and tRNA quantification modules will be disabled.

| Resource | Link |
|----------|------|
| GitHub repository | [https://github.com/boulardlab/3t-seq](https://github.com/boulardlab/3t-seq) |
| Manual | [https://boulardlab.github.io/3t-seq](https://boulardlab.github.io/3t-seq) |

> **Exercise:** Before we start, browse the 3t-seq GitHub repository and
> manual. Try to answer the following questions:
>
> 1. What are the main steps of the pipeline?
> 2. What file does the pipeline use for configuration?
> 3. What format does the sample sheet need to be in?
> 4. What genome assemblies and annotation types does the pipeline support?

## 1.3 What is Snakemake?

3t-seq is built on top of [Snakemake](https://snakemake.readthedocs.io) — a workflow management system that
defines analysis pipelines as a set of rules, where each rule describes how to
produce a specific output from a specific input. Very analogous to **Nextflow** (see `day1_roadmap`, section 4.2).

Snakemake automatically:

- Determines which steps need to be run based on what outputs already exist
- Runs independent steps in parallel
- Retries failed steps
- Keeps a detailed log of everything that was run

---

# 2. Initializing the environment for the pipeline

To configure the 3t-seq, please refer to the [manual](https://boulardlab.github.io/3t-seq) explaining the steps. Below you can find the compressed instructions.

## 2.1 Install pixi
The 3t-seq pipeline uses [pixi](https://pixi.sh) for managing its software
environments. Pixi is a fast, cross-platform package manager built on top of
the conda ecosystem that handles all tool dependencies automatically.

Navigate to directory `~/multimodal-ngs-ubds-2026/pipelines/rna-seq-zone-specific-3t-seq` and run the following commands:

```{bash}
curl -fsSL https://pixi.sh/install.sh | bash
source ~/.bashrc
pixi --version
```

## 2.2 Initializing Git LFS 

The 3t-seq pipeline uses **Git LFS** (Large File Storage) to manage large binary
files that would otherwise bloat the git repository. Without git-lfs installed,
these files will not be downloaded correctly during cloning.

Install git-lfs (for Ubuntu/Debian):
```{bash}
sudo apt install git-lfs
```

And activate it globally (once per machine):
```{bash}
git lfs install
```

## 2.3 Clone the 3t-seq repository

Clone the 3t-seq repository using the command below. Note that we will be working with the development version. 

```{bash}
cd ~/multimodal_ngs_course_ubds_2026/pipelines/rna-seq-zone-specific-3t-seq
git clone -b dev https://github.com/boulardlab/3t-seq.git
cd 3t-seq
```

## 2.4 Install the pipeline environment

Install the `dev` pixi environment, which contains Snakemake and all required
dependencies.

```{bash}
pixi add --feature dev snakemake-executor-plugin-slurm
pixi install -e dev
```

Verify that Snakemake is available:

```{bash}
pixi run -e dev snakemake --version
```

---

# 3. Configuring the pipeline

## 3.1 Overview of configuration files

The pipeline requires three types of configuration:

1. **`samplesheet.csv`** - one CSV file per pairwise comparison, listing samples and their FASTQ paths
2. **`config.yaml`** - global pipeline settings: comparisons, genome, trimming parameters
3. (optional) profile **`config.yaml`** - resource allocation and general computing instructions for the pipeline execution

## 3.2 Set up the sample sheets

We will run two pairwise comparisons: Zone 1 vs Zone 2, Zone 1 vs Zone 5. Each needs its own sample sheet.

Create the sample sheets directory inside of the `3t-seq` folder:
```{bash}
mkdir sample_sheets
```

Create 2 `.csv` files inside of the `sample_sheets` directory:
```{bash}
touch zone1_vs_zone2_samplesheet.csv
touch zone1_vs_zone5_samplesheet.csv
```


Each sample sheet (a CSV file) has the following columns:

| Column | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `name` | Sample name |
| `filename_1` | Path to R1 FASTQ file |
| `filename_2` | Path to R2 FASTQ file |
| `zone` (can be anything, e.g. `genotype`) | Group variable for DESeq2 |

Example: [https://boulardlab.github.io/3t-seq/tutorial/quickstart/#the-sample-sheet]()https://boulardlab.github.io/3t-seq/tutorial/quickstart/#the-sample-sheet

**Task:** Fill out the sample sheets for 2 pairwise comparisons of our zonal dataset and save them in the `3t-seq/sample_sheets` directory.

> **Solution:** Can be found in `solutions/sample_sheets` folder in `~/multimodal-ngs-ubds-2026/pipelines/rna-seq-zone-specific-3t-seq/`. But I highly recommend you to try setting them up on your own to get a real pipeliner experience :) 

```{bash, echo=FALSE, include=FALSE}
#!/bin/bash
# Solution: create all three sample sheets

FASTQ_DIR="/path/to/fastq_renamed"
SHEET_DIR="sample_sheets"
mkdir -p $SHEET_DIR

# zone1_vs_zone2
cat > $SHEET_DIR/zone1_vs_zone2_samplesheet.csv << EOF
sample,fq1,fq2,zone
RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep1,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep1_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep1_R2.fastq.gz,zone1
RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep2,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep2_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep2_R2.fastq.gz,zone1
RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep3,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep3_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep3_R2.fastq.gz,zone1
RNA_Zone2_mOSN_Rep1,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone2_mOSN_Rep1_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone2_mOSN_Rep1_R2.fastq.gz,zone2
RNA_Zone2_mOSN_Rep2,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone2_mOSN_Rep2_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone2_mOSN_Rep2_R2.fastq.gz,zone2
EOF

# zone1_vs_zone5
cat > $SHEET_DIR/zone1_vs_zone5_samplesheet.csv << EOF
sample,fq1,fq2,zone
RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep1,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep1_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep1_R2.fastq.gz,zone1
RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep2,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep2_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep2_R2.fastq.gz,zone1
RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep3,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep3_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone1_mOSN_Rep3_R2.fastq.gz,zone1
RNA_Zone5_mOSN_Rep1,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone5_mOSN_Rep1_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone5_mOSN_Rep1_R2.fastq.gz,zone5
RNA_Zone5_mOSN_Rep2,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone5_mOSN_Rep2_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone5_mOSN_Rep2_R2.fastq.gz,zone5
RNA_Zone5_mOSN_Rep3,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone5_mOSN_Rep3_R1.fastq.gz,$FASTQ_DIR/RNA_Zone5_mOSN_Rep3_R2.fastq.gz,zone5
EOF

echo "Sample sheets created in $SHEET_DIR/"
```

## 3.3 Edit the main `config.yaml`

Open the config file from `3t-seq/config/config.yaml` in VS Code (or a text editor of your choice). E.g.:

```{bash}
nano config/config.yaml
# or
vim config/config.yaml
```

Following the 3t-seq manual, try to configure the `config.yaml` yourself. 

> **Solution:** Can be found in `solutions/config` folder in `~/multimodal-ngs-ubds-2026/pipelines/rna-seq-zone-specific-3t-seq/`. But, again, try to put it together on your own first.

```{yaml, echo=FALSE, include=FALSE}
comparisons:
  - name: zone1_vs_zone2
    protocol: pe
    sample_sheet: /path/to/multimodal-ngs-ubds-2026/pipelines/rna-seq-zone-specific-3t-seq/3t-seq/sample_sheets/zone1_vs_zone2_samplesheet.csv
    strandedness: 0
    trimmomatic: "ILLUMINACLIP:$CONDA_PREFIX/share/trimmomatic/adapters/TruSeq3-PE.fa:2:30:10 CROP:50 LEADING:3 TRAILING:3 SLIDINGWINDOW:4:15 MINLEN:36"
    deseq2:
      test: Wald
      variable: zone
      reference_level: zone2
    
  - name: zone1_vs_zone5
    protocol: pe
    sample_sheet: /path/to/multimodal-ngs-ubds-2026/pipelines/rna-seq-zone-specific-3t-seq/3t-seq/sample_sheets/zone1_vs_zone5_samplesheet.csv
    strandedness: 0
    trimmomatic: "ILLUMINACLIP:$CONDA_PREFIX/share/trimmomatic/adapters/TruSeq3-PE.fa:2:30:10 CROP:50 LEADING:3 TRAILING:3 SLIDINGWINDOW:4:15 MINLEN:36"
    deseq2:
      test: Wald
      variable: zone
      reference_level: zone5

  - name: zone2_vs_zone5
    protocol: pe
    sample_sheet: /path/to/multimodal-ngs-ubds-2026/pipelines/rna-seq-zone-specific-3t-seq/3t-seq/sample_sheets/zone2_vs_zone5_samplesheet.csv
    strandedness: 0
    trimmomatic: "ILLUMINACLIP:$CONDA_PREFIX/share/trimmomatic/adapters/TruSeq3-PE.fa:2:30:10 CROP:50 LEADING:3 TRAILING:3 SLIDINGWINDOW:4:15 MINLEN:36"
    deseq2:
      test: Wald
      variable: zone
      reference_level: zone5


disable_TE_analysis: true
disable_salmonTE_analysis: true
disable_tRNA_analysis: true

globals:
  results_folder: /path/to/multimodal-ngs-ubds-2026/pipelines/rna-seq-zone-specific-3t-seq/3t-seq/results

genome:
  label: mm10
  annotation_type: gencode
```

## 3.4 Setting up the execution profile

The pipeline uses a **Snakemake profile** to configure how jobs are executed.
The profile controls things like the number of parallel jobs, resource requests,
and the container system used.

Create your own profile directory:

```{bash}
mkdir -p 3t-seq/profile/slurm-ubds
```

Create `profile/slurm-ubds/config.yaml` with the following content:

```{yaml}
cores: 32
local-cores: 32
jobs: 4
restart-times: 2
printshellcmds: True
software-deployment-method:
  - apptainer
  - conda
apptainer-args: "--no-home"
conda-frontend: conda
latency-wait: 60
```

> **Note:** We set `jobs: 4` to run up to 4 Snakemake jobs in parallel, and
> `cores: 32` to give Snakemake a total of 32 cores to distribute. 

---

# 4. Launching the pipeline

## 4.1 Update `run.sh`

Create `run.sh` file in your `3t-seq/` directory:

```{bash}
touch run.sh
chmod +x run.sh
```

Paste the commands from below to your `run.sh` file. Make sure the paths are correctly pointed to your execution profile and main config:

```{bash}
pixi run -e dev snakemake \
    --profile profile/slurm-ubds \
    --configfile config/config.yaml \
    --rerun-incomplete
    #--report report-$(date -I).zip
```

## 4.2 Do a dry run first

Before launching for real, do a **dry run** to check that Snakemake can resolve
the full pipeline without errors:

```{bash}
pixi run -e dev snakemake \
    --profile profile/config.yaml \
    --configfile config/config.yaml \
    --dry-run
```

This prints all the jobs that will be executed without actually running them.
Review the output and make sure it looks sensible.

> **Question:** How many jobs does Snakemake plan to run? What are the first steps?

## 4.3 Launch the 3t-seq

Once the dry run looks good, open a screen session and launch the pipeline:

```{bash}
screen -S 3t-seq

./run.sh

# Detach — the pipeline will keep running in the background
# Ctrl + A, then D
```

## 4.4 Monitor progress

```{bash}
# Reattach to the screen
screen -r 3t-seq

# Check which steps have completed
ls results/

# Check the Snakemake log
tail -f .snakemake/log/*.snakemake.log

# Check disk usage
du -sh results/
```

If a step fails, check the log for that specific rule:

```{bash}
# Check the Snakemake log for errors
grep "Error\|error\|failed" .snakemake/log/*.snakemake.log | head -20
```

To rerun only incomplete or failed steps:

```{bash}
pixi run -e dev snakemake \
    --profile profile/local \
    --configfile config/config.yaml \
    --rerun-incomplete
```

---

# Summary of today

| Step | Tool | Output |
|------|------|--------|
| Pipeline introduction | — | Understanding of 3t-seq and Snakemake |
| Environment setup | pixi | 3t-seq environments |
| Repository clone | git + git-lfs | 3t-seq code |
| Sample sheets | CSV files | Per-comparison sample lists |
| Pipeline configuration | config.yaml | Comparison definitions |
| Profile configuration | config.yaml | Resource allocation |
| Pipeline execution | Snakemake | Alignments, counts, QC reports, etc. |

---

# Next steps

Tomorrow we will explore the 3t-seq results, including QC reports and DESeq2 insights.

---

*By Lisa Korneeva with some help of Claude Sonnet 4.6*
